literal: putting-together, collectedness, complete composure of mind, meditative absorption; from sam + ā + √dhā 'to place, to set down together'
invariant_definition: *Samādhi* is the gathered, fully composed condition of the mind in which attention is collected upon a single object or, in the highest forms, rests without object in undivided awareness. The etymology is exact: a 'placing-together' of the otherwise scattered cognitive faculties. Beyond this shared core the term carries sharply different technical loads. In Pātañjala Yoga it is the eighth and culminating limb of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* and the title of the whole first *pāda*; it is analyzed into *samprajñāta* (cognitive, object-bearing absorption) and *asamprajñāta* (the supracognitive absorption in which even the seed of cognition is stilled), and it is the proximate means to *kaivalya*. In Advaita the term is taken over and subdivided into *savikalpa* and *nirvikalpa samādhi*, and a genuine debate arises over its soteriological status: whether *nirvikalpa samādhi* is necessary for, identical with, or merely auxiliary to *brahma-jñāna* — the worry being that *samādhi* is a temporary mental state that ends when one rises from it, whereas liberating knowledge, once arisen, is permanent and not lost on returning to ordinary awareness. In the *Bhagavad-Gītā* the word appears in the *sthitaprajña* passage in a looser but pointed sense: the intellect rendered steady and unmoved, fixed in equanimity (2.53–54, *samādhau acalā buddhiḥ*). The term therefore sits at the intersection of a precise yogic technology of absorption and the Vedāntic question of whether absorption is the same thing as knowing the real.

school_framing.shared_core: *Samādhi* names **the collected, undistracted condition of the mind gathered upon its object or resting objectless in undivided awareness**. The structural role — culminating concentration — is shared. Schools disagree on whether it is the technical eighth limb yielding *kaivalya* (Yoga), a state that may accompany but does not by itself constitute liberating knowledge (much of Advaita), or, more loosely, the steadiness of the equanimous intellect (Gītā).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** within Advaita and against Yoga over the status of object-less absorption: whether *nirvikalpa samādhi* is required for or identical with *brahma-jñāna*, or whether knowledge — being permanent and self-revealing — does not depend on a temporary mental state at all. **Same concept, different aspect** between Yoga's technical *samādhi* and the Gītā's *samādhi* as steadiness of *buddhi*: both name mental collectedness, at different degrees of technical specification.

per_school.definition: *Samādhi* is the eighth limb of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* and the consummation of *saṃyama* (the triad *dhāraṇā–dhyāna–samādhi* taken together, YS 3.1-4); in *samādhi* the object alone shines forth as if emptied of the cognizing act's own form (YS 3.3). It is divided into *samprajñāta*, object-bearing absorption attended by reasoning, reflection, bliss, and I-sense (YS 1.17), and *asamprajñāta*, the higher absorption that follows the cessation of all cognitive content, leaving only latent impressions (YS 1.18). It is the direct means by which the *vṛttis* are stilled and *vivekakhyāti* ripens toward *kaivalya*; the whole first *pāda* is the *samādhi-pāda*.
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; yogin (eighth limb, means to kaivalya)

per_school.definition: Advaita adopts *samādhi* and divides it into *savikalpa* (absorption still bearing the subtle distinction of knower, known, and knowing) and *nirvikalpa* (absorption free of that triadic distinction). Its soteriological status is genuinely debated: one strand treats *nirvikalpa samādhi* as a necessary discipline for steadying the mind so that *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* and *brahma-jñāna* may arise; another insists that liberation is by knowledge alone (*jñānād eva mokṣaḥ*), and that *samādhi* — being a state one enters and leaves — cannot itself be liberation, since *brahma-jñāna* once arisen is permanent and unaffected by the return to ordinary cognition. The persistent worry is the temporariness of any absorbed state versus the permanence of realized identity.
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu [REAL-DISAGREEMENT: nirvikalpa samādhi vs jñāna]

per_school.definition: Classical Sāṅkhya does not make *samādhi* a load-bearing technical category in the way Yoga does; its soteriology turns on discriminative knowledge (*viveka*) that distinguishes *puruṣa* from *prakṛti*, after which *prakṛti* withdraws and *kaivalya* follows. Insofar as the term is used, it names the sattvic concentration of the *buddhi* that makes such discrimination possible — but the operative cause of release is the knowledge itself, not the absorbed state as such. The contemplative apparatus is shared with its sister-system Yoga, while the explanatory weight is placed on *jñāna* rather than on *samādhi*.
primary_loci: Sāṅkhya-Kārikā 64-66 (viveka and the withdrawal of prakṛti)
per_school.register_tag: M+E; reflective reader (samādhi subordinate to viveka)

per_school.definition: In the *Gītā*'s *sthitaprajña* passage *samādhi* is used not for trance-absorption but for the intellect rendered steady and unshaken — *yadā te buddhir ... samādhau acalā* (2.53), the *buddhi* immovably established (2.54, *samādhi-sthasya*). The accent is on equanimity and the withdrawal of the intellect from the pull of competing sense-impressions, rather than on a graded technology of absorptive states. This usage feeds the wider *Gītā* identification of yoga with equanimity (*samatvaṃ yoga ucyate*, 2.48).
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (samādhi as steadiness of buddhi)

translator_note: 'Trance' is misleading and 'meditation' too broad. In Yoga *samādhi* is a precise technical stage (the eighth limb), not meditation in general; in Advaita it names savikalpa/nirvikalpa absorption whose relation to liberating knowledge is itself contested; in the *Gītā* sthitaprajña verses it means the steadiness of the intellect, not a trance state. Do not let the English imply that absorption is automatically liberation — much of Advaita denies exactly that, holding that knowledge, not the temporary absorbed state, is what frees.
